evals.input: I have 5 test files failing. Let me dispatch an agent for each one.
evals.expected_behavior: Stop and check: are the failures independent? Multiple test files failing after a refactor may share a root cause. Investigate first — fixing one root cause may fix all 5. Only parallelize after confirming independence.
evals.anti_pattern: Immediately dispatching 5 agents without checking if failures share a root cause

evals.input: I want to dispatch agents to fix the login page and the user settings page at the same time
evals.expected_behavior: Run the independence test: do they share files? (shared auth module, shared user model?) Does settings depend on login? If they share components or models, they're not independent. Check before dispatching.
evals.anti_pattern: Assuming pages are independent because they have different URLs, without checking for shared code

evals.input: Fix these 3 failing tests: auth.test.ts (line 42), auth.test.ts (line 87), auth.test.ts (line 103)
evals.expected_behavior: Don't parallelize — all 3 failures are in the SAME file. They likely share root cause or context. One agent handles all 3 tests in auth.test.ts. Parallel dispatch is for independent problem domains, not individual tests in one file.
evals.anti_pattern: Dispatching 3 agents for 3 tests in the same file

evals.input: I need to research market trends, competitive analysis, and customer feedback — all for different product areas. Should I dispatch parallel agents?
evals.expected_behavior: Yes — research tasks on unrelated topics are textbook independence. Each agent can work with its own context, no shared state, no file overlap. Use SCCO framework: specific topic scope, relevant context, constraints on depth, output format.
evals.anti_pattern: Running all research sequentially when topics are truly independent

evals.input: Two of my parallel agents both modified the utils.ts file. Now there are merge conflicts.
evals.expected_behavior: This should have been caught by the independence test. The agents weren't truly independent. For now: resolve manually by understanding both changes. For next time: check for file overlap before dispatching. If agents must touch the same file, sequence them instead.
evals.anti_pattern: Re-dispatching the agents without addressing the root problem (shared file dependency)

evals.input: I want to dispatch 10 agents to parallelize all my work
evals.expected_behavior: Push back: beyond 5-6 parallel agents, integration overhead (reviewing, merging, conflict-checking) exceeds time savings. Batch into 3-5 focused groups. 10 agents means reviewing 10 summaries and checking 45 potential conflict pairs.

evals.input: One of my 3 parallel agents failed but the other 2 succeeded. What do I do?
evals.expected_behavior: Don't let one failure block the others. Review and integrate the 2 successful agents' work. Re-dispatch the failed task — possibly with a better prompt, narrower scope, or more context. Check if the failure reveals a hidden dependency with the other tasks.
evals.anti_pattern: Discarding all 3 agents' work because one failed

evals.input: I'm not sure what's broken in the application. Let me dispatch agents to investigate different areas simultaneously.
evals.expected_behavior: Don't parallelize exploratory debugging. If you don't know what's broken, you can't create focused agent scopes. Investigate first to identify specific problems, THEN parallelize the fixes once you know they're independent.
evals.anti_pattern: Dispatching agents with vague scopes like 'investigate the backend' when you haven't identified the actual problems
